---
title: "On Comparing Parameters Across Models with Differing Small-Scale Structure"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

The sequence of models considered across Questions 1 through 4 is not a collection of competing alternatives — it is a progression, each step motivated by what the previous step reveals. Keeping this in mind is essential for interpreting the comparisons that follow, because the natural instinct to compare confidence intervals across these models, while reasonable on its surface, is subtler than it first appears.

Two constraints together shape every comparison in this analysis, and both must be named before proceeding. The first is that we have a single realization of the process. Every estimate in every question — Q1 through Q4 — is conditioned on one trajectory through the data-generating mechanism, and the asymptotic theory invoked throughout concerns $n \to \infty$ along that single path, not repeated independent experiments. This matters beyond the small-$n$ concern already noted in the problem statement. A frequentist confidence interval is a statement about what would happen across repeated realizations of the data-generating process, but we have only one — so the intervals are not just model-conditional, noted below, but additionally path-conditional. The moment-based estimators of Question 2 are affected differently but no less fundamentally: they rely implicitly on an ergodicity argument, substituting averaging along a single path for ensemble averaging. That substitution requires long realizations and stationarity, and with 23 points it is shaky. Most consequentially, the single realization sharpens the identifiability problem in a way that replication would resolve. The nonlinear mean function has enough flexibility that some of what is "really" autocorrelation could be absorbed into $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, and vice versa — and with no replicate trajectories, there is no external anchor to adjudicate between those possibilities.

The second constraint is that the progression across questions does not constitute a nested sequence of models in the sense required for formal model comparison. Questions 1 and 3 appear "superficially nested" — Q1 corresponds to Q3 with $\theta = 0$ — but Q1 was estimated by generalized least squares under the iid assumption, not by maximizing the full likelihood. The likelihood evaluated at the Q1 estimates is therefore not the same object as the likelihood maximized in Q3, and a likelihood ratio test requires both models to be estimated on a common criterion. Further, questions 3 and 4 are not nested at all: The composite likelihood of Q4 is a product of conditional densities that deliberately ignores dependencies across pairs, and it cannot be compared to the full likelihood of Q3 on the same scale. Typical method comparison methods like AIC and BIC, which presuppose a proper log-likelihood, are thus not applicable. And Question 2 is not a full model in any sense — it estimates pieces of the small-scale structure in isolation from surrogate residuals, with no likelihood to compare. Taken together, these two constraints — a single realization and no available formal tests — establish that the motivation-from-diagnostics framing is not merely philosophically preferable. It is the only rigorous path forward. The diagnostic residual pattern from Q1 must carry the full weight of the model-building argument, because there is no formal machinery available to substitute for it.

With those constraints named, the structure of the analysis falls into place. Question 1 fits the extended Freundlich curve under the simplest possible small-scale assumption: iid normal errors with constant variance. This model is not proposed as the truth — it is proposed as a diagnostic tool. Its residual plot, analogous to Figure 3 of Sibbesen (1981), is the primary output of interest, because the pattern in those residuals is what motivates everything that follows. If the residuals exhibited no structure across increasing covariate values, there would be no grounds for introducing an AR(1) error process. But the residual pattern gives us reason to believe the iid assumption is wrong, and that the unexplained variation carries autocorrelation across the ordered covariate values. Question 2 isolates the small-scale structure and asks what can be learned about it from the Q1 residuals as surrogate observations. Question 3 takes the fully motivated model — extended Freundlich mean function with AR(1) errors — and estimates all parameters simultaneously under a coherent likelihood. Question 4 does the same under a composite likelihood. The simpler models are scaffolding. The complex model is the destination.

With that ordering in place, the role of cross-model comparisons becomes clear. Throughout Questions 1 through 4, the large-scale structure is held fixed: The expectation function is always $\mu_i = \alpha x_i^{\beta x_i^{-\gamma}}$, and so $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ carry a consistent scientific interpretation across all formulations. Every estimator considered is targeting the same population quantities, which means that comparisons of point estimates across models are well-posed and genuinely informative — specifically, they function as a sensitivity analysis. Having committed to the AR(1) model on substantive diagnostic grounds, the question we bring to the comparison is: *how much does acknowledging the true error structure move our large-scale conclusions?* Stability in the point estimates of $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ across Q1 and Q3 would be reassuring, suggesting the large-scale fit is not materially distorted by the small-scale misspecification in Q1. Meaningful shifts warrant attribution: some movement is expected because the full likelihood incorporates the dependence structure into the estimation criterion rather than minimizing a sum of squared residuals, but large discrepancies with $n = 23$ and five parameters may also reflect sensitivity of the optimization to the shape of the likelihood surface along this particular realization.

Confidence intervals are a different matter, and this is where the comparison requires the most care. A 95% confidence interval for $\gamma$ from Question 1 is the statement: *assuming iid normal errors, the uncertainty in our estimate of $\gamma$ is as follows.* The analogous interval from Question 3 is: *assuming AR(1) errors, the uncertainty in our estimate of $\gamma$ is as follows.* Because at most one small-scale model can be correct, at most one of these intervals has its advertised coverage. They are not two estimates of the same inferential object — they are answers to different questions, each conditional on its own model. Comparing them directly as if they were commensurable is a category error, made more acute by the single-realization setting, where neither interval has a clean frequentist referent to begin with, and compounded by the absence of formal nesting, which means no test can adjudicate between the models they are premised on.

What the comparison can honestly offer is a directional check: does the uncertainty in the large-scale parameters change in a theoretically predictable way when we move to the motivated model? If $\theta > 0$ — which the residual pattern suggests — positive autocorrelation reduces the effective sample size below $n$. The Q1 intervals, premised on $n$ independent observations, overcount the available information (positively correlated observations are partially redundant and contribute less than a full independent unit of information each) and are anti-conservative (coverage below its nominal level). Accounting for the autocorrelation should widen the confidence intervals for $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$, so wider intervals from Q3 relative to Q1 are a sign that the model is behaving correctly, not a sign of disagreement. The same directional logic governs the small-scale comparisons. The Q1 moment estimate of $\sigma^2$ attributes all unexplained variation to pure error, because the iid assumption leaves no other home for it; if $\theta > 0$, this inflates $\hat{\sigma}^2$ by folding the autocorrelated component into the pure error term. Likewise, the moment-based estimate of $\theta$ from Question 2 is not estimating $\theta$ cleanly — it absorbs whatever residual nonlinearity and parameter estimation error remain in the Q1 surrogate residuals, contamination that is not negligible at $n = 23$ and cannot be averaged away. The Q3 MLE estimates all five parameters jointly, so that the large-scale and small-scale components are not artificially separated, and both $\hat{\theta}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2$ should reflect this. In each case, the estimates across models are not two noisy readings of the same quantity — they are estimating conceptually related but formally distinct objects, and the direction of the differences is theoretically predictable even when the magnitude is not.

This brings us to Question 5 and the appeal of a two-stage procedure. Estimating the large-scale structure first and then recovering the small-scale parameters from the residuals is intuitively natural, and indeed it is the logic by which Questions 1 and 2 build toward the full model. The problem is that this sequence, if treated as a finished inferential procedure rather than as scaffolding, does not propagate uncertainty across stages. The standard errors from Q1 make no use of the subsequently estimated $\theta \neq 0$; they remain premised on iid errors. The $\hat{\theta}$ obtained from surrogate residuals carries its own uncertainty that is never folded back into the large-scale inference. In a replicated setting one might hope to validate the two-stage procedure empirically, or to appeal to an iterated GLS argument in which a consistent first-stage estimate of $\theta$ is used to construct $\hat{\Sigma}^{-1}(\hat{\theta})$ for a second-stage weighted fit, recovering asymptotic efficiency in the limit. The two-stage procedure as implemented here cannot do this, because the first stage fixes $W = I$ rather than iterating. With a single realization of 23 points, a five-parameter nonlinear expectation function, and no formal test available to assess whether the two stages are jointly sufficient, compounding approximation error across stages makes the overall procedure unreliable for inference even if the point estimates are individually reasonable. The joint likelihood of Q3 avoids this by estimating all parameters simultaneously under the model we have been motivated to adopt — and while Q3's own asymptotic warrant is strained at this sample size, it remains the most defensible available option precisely because it is the only procedure here that treats the model as a coherent whole.
